{"action":"opened","number":27746,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27746","id":2176557189,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAA3dP86Bu6CF","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27746","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27746.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27746.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27746","number":27746,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"DOC: Added additional guidance for compiling in Windows","user":{"login":"nullSoup","id":34267803,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM0MjY3ODAz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/34267803?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup","html_url":"https://github.com/nullSoup","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Building numpy on Windows has some pitfalls not explicitly mentioned in the existing documentation. I added some details that would have greatly reduced the timed needed to produce a working build if I had them. They are:

-Added a recommended UCRT version as the version I tried initially was missing stdalign.h
-Noted that MSVC environment variable setup gets handled by conda environment activation if the Visual Studio install includes the VS 2019 C++ x86/x64 build tools (MSVC v142).
-Provided advice on failed build with LNK1107 fatal error caused by meson issue selecting the appropriate build tools similar to the this reported issue:

https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson/issues/10022

It appears that the default build process on Windows creates .a files with llvm-ar and then tries to link them with link.exe, which does not recognize such files. Setting environment variables CC_LD and CXX_LD to lld-link seems to solve the problem. Reviewing np.show_config() for the Windows release build, it appears the linker is link.exe not lld-link, so if it there is an alternative fix that gets the build working with link.exe, I would appreciate any feedback on how it should be Users attempting to build numpy in Windows will likely face roadblocks without additional details. Build process description in:

doc/source/building/index.rst

would benefit from 1) recommending a UCRT version for Windows (not all will work), 2) clarifying that MSVC variables will be set by conda activation of the numpy-dev environment so long as the the right build tools are installed, and 3) giving information on working around a meson bug that selects the wrong build tools on Windows.

### Idea or request for content:

I have created [gh-27746](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27746) to add guidance to the documentation for all these concerns. Any feedback is appreciated, especially regarding the best way to handle the meson problem.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27747/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27747/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

numpy on Windows has some pitfalls not explicitly mentioned in the existing documentation. I added some details that would have greatly reduced the timed needed to produce a working build if I had them. They are:

-Added a recommended UCRT version as the version I tried initially was missing stdalign.h
-Noted that MSVC environment variable setup gets handled by conda environment activation if the Visual Studio install includes the VS 2019 C++ x86/x64 build tools (MSVC v142).
-Provided advice on failed build with LNK1107 fatal error caused by a meson issue causing it to fail to select the appropriate build tools similar to the this reported issue:

https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson/issues/10022

It appears that the default build process on Windows creates .a files with llvm-ar and then tries to link them with link.exe, which does not recognize such files. Setting environment variables CC_LD and CXX_LD to lld-link seems to solve the problem. Reviewing np.show_config() for the Windows release build, it appears the linker is link.exe not lld-link, so if it there is an alternative fix that gets the build working with link.exe, I would appreciate any feedback on how it should be numpy on Windows has some pitfalls not explicitly mentioned in the existing documentation. I added some details that would have greatly reduced the timed needed to produce a working build if I had them. They are:

-Added a recommended UCRT version as the version I tried initially was missing stdalign.h
-Noted that MSVC environment variable setup gets handled by conda environment activation if the Visual Studio install includes the VS 2019 C++ x86/x64 build tools (MSVC v142).
-Provided advice on failed build with LNK1107 fatal error caused by a meson issue causing it to fail to select the appropriate build tools similar to the this reported issue:

https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson/issues/10022

It appears that the default build process on Windows creates .a files with llvm-ar and then tries to link them with link.exe, which does not recognize such files. Setting environment variables CC_LD and CXX_LD to lld-link seems to solve the problem. Reviewing np.show_config() for the Windows release build, it appears the linker is link.exe not lld-link, so if it there is an alternative fix that gets the build working with link.exe, I would appreciate any feedback on how it should be          64-bit Intel CPUs, use ``vcvars64.bat``).

This is extremely nice since this MSVC environment variable setup seems quite complex and confusing if you start trying to actually drive into it. Miniforge mamba also launches in Windows via its own batch file setup, so you can't use the Visual Studio setup command prompt launcher at the same time, at least directly. From my experience, what I want to convey to other people trying to get this working is that if using conda, it should just work. If it doesn't, the issue is likely that the right MSVC build tools version wasn't numpy on Windows has some pitfalls not explicitly mentioned in the existing documentation. I added some details that would have greatly reduced the timed needed to produce a working build if I had them. They are:

-Added a recommended UCRT version as the version I tried initially was missing stdalign.h
-Noted that MSVC environment variable setup gets handled by conda environment activation if the Visual Studio install includes the VS 2019 C++ x86/x64 build tools (MSVC v142).
-Provided advice on failed build with LNK1107 fatal error caused by a meson issue causing it to fail to select the appropriate build tools similar to the this reported issue:

https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson/issues/10022

It appears that the default build process on Windows creates .a files with llvm-ar and then tries to link them with link.exe, which does not recognize such files. Setting environment variables CC_LD and CXX_LD to lld-link seems to solve the problem. Reviewing np.show_config() for the Windows release build, it appears the linker is link.exe not lld-link, so if it there is an alternative fix that gets the build working with link.exe, I would appreciate any feedback on how it should be Per the meson issue link above, this incorrect tool flow selection problem seems to be a meson bug. The workaround I could make work was setting environment variables such that lld-link, which does read .a files, is used instead of link.exe. I don't doubt that this isn't the ideal solution, but I don't understand the meson build process well enough to debug what's going wrong such that llvm-ar is getting involved in a MSVC build flow. Any pointers on where to look to fix this flow more correctly would be much appreciated. Right now, it appears any fresh Windows build setup, at least with Visual Studio 2022 (have not checked other versions), is broken, and the lld-link workaround is the only path I've found to fix #27747.

Building numpy on Windows has some pitfalls not explicitly mentioned in the existing documentation. I added some details that would have greatly reduced the timed needed to produce a working build if I had them. They are:

-Added a recommended UCRT version as the version I tried initially was missing stdalign.h
-Noted that MSVC environment variable setup gets handled by conda environment activation if the Visual Studio install includes the VS 2019 C++ x86/x64 build tools (MSVC v142).
-Provided advice on failed build with LNK1107 fatal error caused by a meson issue causing it to fail to select the appropriate build tools similar to the this reported issue:

https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson/issues/10022

It appears that the default build process on Windows creates .a files with llvm-ar and then tries to link them with link.exe, which does not recognize such files. Setting environment variables CC_LD and CXX_LD to lld-link seems to solve the problem. Reviewing np.show_config() for the Windows release build, it appears the linker is link.exe not lld-link, so if it there is an alternative fix that gets the build working with link.exe, I would appreciate any feedback on how it should be #27747.

Building numpy on Windows has some pitfalls not explicitly mentioned in the existing documentation. I added some details that would have greatly reduced the timed needed to produce a working build if I had them. They are:

-Added a recommended UCRT version as the version I tried initially was missing stdalign.h
-Noted that MSVC environment variable setup gets handled by conda environment activation if the Visual Studio install includes the VS 2019 C++ x86/x64 build tools (MSVC v142).
-Provided advice on failed build with LNK1107 fatal error caused by a meson issue causing it to fail to select the appropriate build tools similar to the this reported issue:

https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson/issues/10022

It appears that the default build process on Windows creates .a files with llvm-ar and then tries to link them with link.exe, which does not recognize such files. Setting environment variables CC_LD and CXX_LD to lld-link seems to solve the problem. Reviewing np.show_config() for the Windows release build, it appears the linker is link.exe not lld-link, so if it there is an alternative fix that gets the build working with link.exe, I would appreciate any feedback on how it should be Looking through the numpy-dev conda activation scripts in numpy-dev/etc/conda/activate.d/, I see flang_win-64_activate.bat sets environment variable AR=llvm-ar.exe. Unsetting this variable fixes the build! The same script also sets LDFLAGS to add a clang specific option which triggers unrecognized options warnings during the build and sets LD=lld-link.exe, which doesn't seem to matter, but shouldn't be there.

This script is getting added via the "compilers" package entry in environment.yml. This package is required to get the vcvars auto-setup to work (and maybe for other reasons), so it shouldn't be removed. As far as I can tell, there isn't a way to specify package options to get a version of compilers without flang. I don't know if there's some other good way to get conda to keep everything required installed but just not run the flang activation scripts?

Assuming no smoother solutions are on offer, I propose that I delete this proposed section and instead add a comment in both the "Building from source to use NumPy" and "Building from source for NumPy development" sections recommending unsetting AR, LDLAGS, and LD before running spin build on #27747.

Building numpy on Windows has some pitfalls not explicitly mentioned in the existing documentation. I added some details that would have greatly reduced the timed needed to produce a working build if I had them. They are:

-Added a recommended UCRT version as the version I tried initially was missing stdalign.h
-Noted that MSVC environment variable setup gets handled by conda environment activation if the Visual Studio install includes the VS 2019 C++ x86/x64 build tools (MSVC v142).
-Provided advice on failed build with LNK1107 fatal error caused by a meson issue causing it to fail to select the appropriate build tools similar to the this reported issue:

https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson/issues/10022

It appears that the default build process on Windows creates .a files with llvm-ar and then tries to link them with link.exe, which does not recognize such files. Setting environment variables CC_LD and CXX_LD to lld-link seems to solve the problem. Reviewing np.show_config() for the Windows release build, it appears the linker is link.exe not lld-link, so if it there is an alternative fix that gets the build working with link.exe, I would appreciate any feedback on how it should be #27747.

Building numpy on Windows has some pitfalls not explicitly mentioned in the existing documentation. I added some details that would have greatly reduced the timed needed to produce a working build if I had them. They are:

-Added a recommended UCRT version as the version I tried initially was missing stdalign.h
-Noted that MSVC environment variable setup gets handled by conda environment activation if the Visual Studio install includes the VS 2019 C++ x86/x64 build tools (MSVC v142).
-Provided advice on failed build with LNK1107 fatal error caused by a meson issue causing it to fail to select the appropriate build tools similar to the this reported issue:

https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson/issues/10022

It appears that the default build process on Windows creates .a files with llvm-ar and then tries to link them with link.exe, which does not recognize such files. Setting environment variables CC_LD and CXX_LD to lld-link seems to solve the problem. Reviewing np.show_config() for the Windows release build, it appears the linker is link.exe not lld-link, so if it there is an alternative fix that gets the build working with link.exe, I would appreciate any feedback on how it should be Looking through the numpy-dev conda activation scripts in numpy-dev/etc/conda/activate.d/, I see flang_win-64_activate.bat sets environment variable AR=llvm-ar.exe. Unsetting this variable fixes the build! The same script also sets LDFLAGS to add a clang specific option which triggers unrecognized options warnings during the build and sets LD=lld-link.exe, which doesn't seem to matter, but shouldn't be there.

This script is getting added via the "compilers" package entry in environment.yml. This package is required to get the vcvars auto-setup to work (and maybe for other reasons), so it shouldn't be removed. As far as I can tell, there isn't a way to specify package options to get a version of compilers without flang. I don't know if there's some other good way to get conda to keep everything required installed but just not run the flang activation scripts?

Assuming no smoother solutions are on offer, I propose that I delete this proposed section and instead add a comment in both the "Building from source to use NumPy" and "Building from source for NumPy development" sections recommending unsetting AR, LDLAGS, and LD before running spin build on #27747.

Building numpy on Windows has some pitfalls not explicitly mentioned in the existing documentation. I added some details that would have greatly reduced the timed needed to produce a working build if I had them. They are:

-Added a recommended UCRT version as the version I tried initially was missing stdalign.h
-Noted that MSVC environment variable setup gets handled by conda environment activation if the Visual Studio install includes the VS 2019 C++ x86/x64 build tools (MSVC v142).
-Provided advice on failed build with LNK1107 fatal error caused by a meson issue causing it to fail to select the appropriate build tools similar to the this reported issue:

https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson/issues/10022

It appears that the default build process on Windows creates .a files with llvm-ar and then tries to link them with link.exe, which does not recognize such files. Setting environment variables CC_LD and CXX_LD to lld-link seems to solve the problem. Reviewing np.show_config() for the Windows release build, it appears the linker is link.exe not lld-link, so if it there is an alternative fix that gets the build working with link.exe, I would appreciate any feedback on how it should be #27747.

Building numpy on Windows has some pitfalls not explicitly mentioned in the existing documentation. I added some details that would have greatly reduced the timed needed to produce a working build if I had them. They are:

-Added a recommended UCRT version as the version I tried initially was missing stdalign.h
-Noted that MSVC environment variable setup gets handled by conda environment activation if the Visual Studio install includes the VS 2019 C++ x86/x64 build tools (MSVC v142).
-Provided advice on failed build with LNK1107 fatal error caused by a meson issue causing it to fail to select the appropriate build tools similar to the this reported issue:

https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson/issues/10022

It appears that the default build process on Windows creates .a files with llvm-ar and then tries to link them with link.exe, which does not recognize such files. Setting environment variables CC_LD and CXX_LD to lld-link seems to solve the problem. Reviewing np.show_config() for the Windows release build, it appears the linker is link.exe not lld-link, so if it there is an alternative fix that gets the build working with link.exe, I would appreciate any feedback on how it should be Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""},{"id":82470144,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw4MjQ3MDE0NA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20build","name":"component: build","color":"009800","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-11-13T06:47:24Z","updated_at":"2024-11-14T04:21:29Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27746","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27746","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27746.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27746.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Fixes #27747.

Building numpy on Windows has some pitfalls not explicitly mentioned in the existing documentation. I added some details that would have greatly reduced the timed needed to produce a working build if I had them. They are:

-Added a recommended UCRT version as the version I tried initially was missing stdalign.h
-Noted that MSVC environment variable setup gets handled by conda environment activation if the Visual Studio install includes the VS 2019 C++ x86/x64 build tools (MSVC v142).
-Provided advice on failed build with LNK1107 fatal error caused by a meson issue causing it to fail to select the appropriate build tools similar to the this reported issue:

https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson/issues/10022

It appears that the default build process on Windows creates .a files with llvm-ar and then tries to link them with link.exe, which does not recognize such files. Setting environment variables CC_LD and CXX_LD to lld-link seems to solve the problem. Reviewing np.show_config() for the Windows release build, it appears the linker is link.exe not lld-link, so if it there is an alternative fix that gets the build working with link.exe, I would appreciate any feedback on how it should be done.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27746/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27746/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2475384252","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27746#issuecomment-2475384252","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27746","id":2475384252,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86Ti128","user":{"login":"nullSoup","id":34267803,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM0MjY3ODAz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/34267803?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup","html_url":"https://github.com/nullSoup","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-14T04:21:28Z","updated_at":"2024-11-14T04:21:28Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Thanks for the feedback! {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27760","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27760/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27760/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27760/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27760","id":2657859950,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86ea7lu","number":27760,"title":"BUG: vectorized ufunc error reporting is inconsistent","user":{"login":"nullSoup","id":34267803,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM0MjY3ODAz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/34267803?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup","html_url":"https://github.com/nullSoup","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 - Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-11-14T07:24:06Z","updated_at":"2024-11-14T07:24:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

Floating point flags raised during execution of ufunc's created via np.vectorize are not caught during execution in a consistent and useful fashion.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import math
import sys
import numpy as np

def my_func_1(x):
    a = math.isinf(np.nan)
    return ''

def my_func_2(x):
    a = math.isinf(np.nan)
    np.any([True])
    return ''

print("in my_func_1()", file=sys.stderr)
np.vectorize(my_func_1)(1)
print("in my_func_2()", file=sys.stderr)
np.vectorize(my_func_2)(1)
```


### Error message:

```shell
******\numpy-test-TRcxodTR-py3.12\Lib\site-packages\numpy\lib\_function_base_impl.py:2553: RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in my_func_1 (vectorized)
  outputs = ufunc(*inputs)
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

2.1.3

print(sys.version):

### Context for the issue:

Typically, RuntimeWarning is useful feedback for np.vectorize() functions as errors are raised when the vectorize'd function encounters invalid inputs. On Windows using python 2.11, math.isinf(np.nan) raises a floating point error (though it should not and does not in Windows builds with python <= 3.10). This error is then cleared if a subsequent numpy unfuc is called (like np.any() in the example), so there is no way for code that calls numpy.vectorize functions to ensure that seterr() handling captures all floating point errors that arise during execution of the function.

Background: isinf() is called by hash() in python 3.10, and in python 3.11 this sets a floating point exception if the argument is a NaN in Windows builds. This issue was found because the hash() function is called by the pandas.loc() machinery, through which a call to hash(NaN) is invoked through the use of the functools.lru_cache decorator. While python 3.11 introduces a number of updates related to the handling of  NaNs, it is unclear what change introduces the issue, and the problem does not appear in non-Windows builds, so it may be that the root cause is in cpython or the Windows ucrt runtime. If that is the case, I would appreciate feedback on on how to trace issues with  the floating point error flags given that (I believe) cpython does not check them, so I am not sure what a correct test case is to trace the issue in cpython. Regardless, it appears that compound calls to numpy ufuncs clear the floating point flags, so there is no guarantee that the np.seterr() handling is ever invoked, and the numpy.seterr() machinery may or may not be invoked if numpy.vectorize functions generate floating point errors. Not sure if this should be fixed via machinery to capture and retain errors subsequently clearly or if the documentation should be updated to clarify in what cases floating point errors will be routed to the np.seterr() machinery.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27760/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27760/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Floating point flags raised during execution of ufunc's created via np.vectorize are not caught during execution in a consistent and useful fashion.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import math
import sys
import numpy as np

def my_func_1(x):
    a = math.isinf(np.nan)
    return ''

def my_func_2(x):
    a = math.isinf(np.nan)
    np.any([True])
    return ''

print("in my_func_1()", file=sys.stderr)
np.vectorize(my_func_1)(1)
print("in my_func_2()", file=sys.stderr)
np.vectorize(my_func_2)(1)
```

in my_func_2()

### Context for the issue:

Typically, RuntimeWarning is useful feedback for np.vectorize() functions as errors are raised when the vectorize'd function encounters invalid inputs. On Windows using python 2.11, math.isinf(np.nan) raises a floating point error (though it should not and does not in Windows builds with python <= 3.10). This error is then cleared if a subsequent numpy unfuc is called (like np.any() in the example), so there is no way for code that calls numpy.vectorize functions to ensure that seterr() handling captures all floating point errors that arise during execution of the function.

Background: isinf() is called by hash() in python 3.10, and in python 3.11 this sets a floating point exception if the argument is a NaN in Windows builds. This issue was found because the hash() function is called by the pandas.loc() machinery, through which a call to hash(NaN) is invoked through the use of the functools.lru_cache decorator. While python 3.11 introduces a number of updates related to the handling of  NaNs, it is unclear what change introduces the issue, and the problem does not appear in non-Windows builds, so it may be that the root cause is in cpython or the Windows ucrt runtime. If that is the case, I would appreciate feedback on on how to trace issues with  the floating point error flags given that (I believe) cpython does not check them, so I am not sure what a correct test case is to trace the issue in cpython. Regardless, it appears that compound calls to numpy ufuncs clear the floating point flags, so there is no guarantee that the np.seterr() handling is ever invoked, and the numpy.seterr() machinery may or may not be invoked if numpy.vectorize functions generate floating point errors. Not sure if this should be fixed via machinery to capture and retain errors subsequently clearly or if the documentation should be updated to clarify in what cases floating point errors will be routed to the np.seterr() machinery.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27760/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27760/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2477513941","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27760#issuecomment-2477513941","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27760","id":2477513941,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86Tq9zV","user":{"login":"nullSoup","id":34267803,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM0MjY3ODAz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/34267803?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup","html_url":"https://github.com/nullSoup","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullSoup/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-14T22:15:11Z","updated_at":"2024-11-14T22:15:11Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Thanks! Do I understand correctly that you are saying that the most appropriate fix would be to make ufunc's created via vectorize() simply not report any floating point errors? As long as added to vectorize's documentation, I think that makes sense as users have the option to do their own error checking in their vectorize()ed function.

I can volunteer to create a PR with this change, though I may possibly be getting a bit outside my depth.

It looks like I would need to modify ufunc_frompyfunc() which goes through the below call chain to where this flag can be set:

PyUFunc_FromFuncDataAndSignatureAndIdentity() -> add_and_return_legacy_wrapping_ufunc_loop() -> PyArray_NewLegacyWrappingArrayMethod()

Not sure what the most appropriate way to pass the info that we want to set NPY_METH_NO_FLOAINGPOINT_ERRORS would be. Should I create superset versions of each of these three functions adding a bool (npy_bool?) argument for the state of this flag or is this creating too much of a mess? the significance of avoiding these warnings, I use vectorize with pandas frequently, and in the past these warnings have showed up when I passed data that my vectorized function didn't anticipate, so it was useful feedback. After updating to Python 3.11, I encountered the warning and assumed that must have been the problem. Only after chasing my tail for way too long did I realize the issue wasn't actually my code or data. In fact, the pandas loc code is written such that whether you get the warning or not depends on the dtype of the last column in your dataframe, which encouraged the tail chasing as it seemed like the warning was based on what data was fed to the function.  All of which to say, while it would be nice if FPE checking could be preserved in a way where the warnings were always preserved and then raised for any FPE that came up, I gather that would be complicated to implement and probably not worth it. Just not raising FPEs for python callback functions where the floating point flags may be set and cleared in a uncontrolled way during execution would save a great deal of confusion for those of us unlucky enough to run into a bad case. In the interest of sanity preservation for other future users who stumble on this, I'd like to help achieve that to the extent that my skills are sufficient.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2484052108/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Doing more digging, the new API plan doesn't seem to quite work, at least not if used in the same manner that it is used in string_ufuncs.cpp.

For those ufuncs, it appears that generate_umath.py produces __umath_generated.c which does the initial ufunc creation with the same PyUFunc_FromFuncAndDataAndSignatureAndIdentity() function already being used to create the ufunc for vectorize(). The code in string_ufuncs.cpp loads these already created ufuncs and adds more ArrayMethods to the ufunc->_loops with the new API PyUFunc_AddLoopFromSpec().

If we used that approach in this case, PyUFunc_FromFuncAndDataAndSignatureAndIdentity() would already create the needed ArrayMethod, and trying to add it again except with the correct flags via PyUFunc_AddLoopFromSpec() would fail, as it would be a duplicate (which would be fully blocked by the call to PyUFunc_AddLoop() with ignore_duplicate=0).

Unless there is an existing method to create a ufunc like the ones generated by PyUFunc_FromFuncAndDataAndSignatureAndIdentity() except skipping the step where the ArrayMethod is loaded into _loops, I think the task is to write one, which leads to something quite similar to my first attempted PR.

I think the main problem is just that we don't want to change the public API, right? If so,  just deleting the /*UFUNC_API*/ tag from PyUFunc_FromFuncAndDataAndSignatureAndIdentityAndFlags() ought to do the trick, but for whatever reason, this results in python crashing when a vectorized function is called. I think the problem must have to do with special handling of ufunc_object.c in the build, as moving the function prototype into dispatching.h corrects the problem. I imagine that this is not the right answer, so I'd welcome alternatives and any other This implemention adds no new functions, but maybe it is bad that it instead duplicates some of the code in PyArray_NewLegacyWrappingArrayMethod instead?

I suppose you could make a case that it would better to just add the ability to add more flexibility, including the ability to set the NPY_METH_NO_FLOATINGPOINT_ERRORS flag, in that function. From what I can tell, at least internally, that function is only ever called from add_and_return_legacy_wrapping_ufunc_loop, so maybe this could even be done by simply adding an argument and not having to introduce a variant of the function? For that matter, since it only has the one use, the functions could even be combined. The comment immediately above PyArray_NewLegacyWrappingArrayMethod makes me think that may have been the original idea?

Or maybe the implementation should go away from using the legacy_array_method stuff entirely?

Just trying to fix the issue in the narrowest way possible, but I don't have the big picture to judge. Whatever makes the most sense.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2494968717/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
